cases.title: RARE: Retrieval-Aware Robustness Evaluation
cases.research_question: How robust are RAG systems under query, document, and retrieval perturbations?

revisions.statement: Leveraging this pipeline, we construct a dataset (RARE-Set) spanning 527 expert-level time-sensitive finance, economics, and policy documents and 48295 questions whose distribution evolves as the underlying sources change.
revisions.falsifiable_condition: A matched evaluation does not reproduce the reported result.
revisions.source_quote:
Leveraging this pipeline, we construct a dataset (RARE-
Set) spanning 527 expert-level time-sensitive finance, economics, and policy documents
and 48295 questions whose distribution evolves as the underlying sources change.

contract.scope: 527 documents, 48,295 questions across finance, economics, policy; time-sensitive with dynamic updates

revisions.statement: Our findings reveal that RAG systems are unexpectedly sensitive to perturbations.
revisions.falsifiable_condition: A matched evaluation does not reproduce the reported result.
revisions.source_quote:
Our findings reveal that
RAG systems are unexpectedly sensitive to perturbations.

contract.scope: Finance, economics, policy; all perturbation types

revisions.statement: Moreover, they consistently demonstrate lower robustness on multi-hop queries compared to single-hop queries across all domains.
revisions.falsifiable_condition: A matched evaluation does not reproduce the reported result.
revisions.source_quote:
Moreover, they consistently
demonstrate lower robustness on multi-hop queries compared to single-hop queries
across all domains.
1.

revisions.statement: Our evaluation reveals that RAG systems are still fragile under some perturbations.
revisions.falsifiable_condition: A matched evaluation does not reproduce the reported result.
revisions.source_quote: Our evaluation reveals that RAG systems are still fragile under some perturbations.

revisions.statement: Robustness scores do not always scale strictly with model size - some mid-sized generators outperform several larger counterparts.
revisions.falsifiable_condition: A matched evaluation does not reproduce the reported result.

Robustness
scores do not always scale strictly with model size - some mid-sized generators outperform several

revisions.statement: GPT-4.1 achieves a robustness score that surpasses those of its smaller models, GPT-4.1 mini and GPT-4.1 nano.
revisions.falsifiable_condition: A matched evaluation does not reproduce the reported result.

GPT-4.1 achieves a robustness score that surpasses those of its smaller
models, GPT-4.1 mini and GPT-4.1 nano.

revisions.statement: A similar scaling-law is observed within the Llama 3.2 series: Llama-3.2-90B-Vision-Instruct exhibits a markedly higher robustness score than any other model.
revisions.falsifiable_condition: A matched evaluation does not reproduce the reported result.

A similar scaling-law is observed within the Llama 3.2
series: Llama-3.2-90B-Vision-Instruct exhibits a markedly higher robustness score than any other
model.

revisions.statement: For example, Qwen3-32B attains an overall robustness score lower than that of the smaller - but architecturally similar -Qwen3-8B and even Qwen3-4B.

For example, Qwen3-32B attains an overall robustness
score lower than that of the smaller - but architecturally similar - Qwen3-8B and even Qwen3-4B.

revisions.statement: The Qwen3 models consistently maintain a relatively high robustness score, even for smaller-scale variants such as the 4B model.
revisions.falsifiable_condition: A matched evaluation does not reproduce the reported result.

The Qwen3 models
consistently maintain a relatively high robustness score, even for smaller-scale variants such as the

revisions.statement: In addition, compared with other robustness scores, the document score does not exhibit a significant improvement as model size increases; in fact, some models even show regression.
revisions.falsifiable_condition: A matched evaluation does not reproduce the reported result.
revisions.source_quote:
In addition, compared with other robustness scores, the document score does not exhibit
a significant improvement as model size increases; in fact, some models even show regression.

contract.scope: Finance, economics, policy domains; document perturbations: lexical-sim without answer, lexical-diff with answer, ground truth, real-world retrieval

In addition, single-hop queries consistently
yield higher robustness scores than multi-hop queries across all domains and perturbations (Figure

impacts.claim_statement: Our findings reveal that RAG systems are unexpectedly sensitive to perturbations.

source.title: The Powerless Noise: How Experimental Settings Shape the Reported Power of Noise
source.venue: Proceedings of the 49th International ACM SIGIR Conference on Research and Development in Information Retrieval

impacts.source_snapshot_abstract: Recent work has suggested that adding irrelevant documents to the input of retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems can improve question-answering performance, a phenomenon referred to as the Power of Noise. This motivated investigations into the role of noise in information retrieval. In this paper, we reproduce the main findings of Cuconasu et al. and evaluate the robustness of the effect under extended experimental settings. We first confirm that the phenomenon holds under the original setup, which uses earlier-generation LLMs, restrictive prompting and constrained decoding settings. We subsequently introduce a series of extensions to investigate the underlying causes of the noise effect, examining the authors' original design choices including the use of different models, instruction prompting, and relaxed output length constraints. Across these ablations, the Power-of-Noise pattern proves highly sensitive to inference configuration: it can appear, weaken, or disappear under small changes to prompt formulation and decoding limits. Combined with our error analysis, which shows substantial contributions from truncation and malformed generations, this variance indicates that the original effect cannot be robustly confirmed as a general benefit of noisy retrieval under these experimental conditions. More broadly, our work highlights the importance of carefully scrutinizing inference design in retrieval-augmented generation systems. Our code is available at https://github.com/ina0105/The-Power-of-Noise-Reproduction.

source.title: MIRAGE: Defending Long-Form RAG Against Misinformation Pollution

impacts.source_snapshot_abstract: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) improves factuality by grounding LLMs in external evidence, but real-world retrieval is often polluted: semantically relevant passages may contain subtle misinformation, misleading framings, or fabrications. We introduce MIRAGE, a training-free, model-agnostic defense for long-form RAG. MIRAGE builds an NLI-based cross-document claim graph and applies a Defended-Claims Gate to either condition generation on a consistent, multi-source supported subset or to block retrieval and answer parametrically. We also release a minimal-edit pollution protocol spanning four perturbation families (Unambiguous, Conflicting, Misleading, Fabricated) to construct matched clean, mixed, and fully polluted evaluation regimes. Across four long-form QA benchmarks and multiple commercial and open-weight LLMs, pollution severely degrades vanilla RAG, while MIRAGE consistently restores factuality under mixed and fully polluted evidence and outperforms prior robust-RAG methods. Our implementation and datasets are available at https://github.com/SaadElDine/MIRAGE.

source.title: Efficient RAG with Intent-Aware Retrieval and Semantics-Preserving Chunking

impacts.source_snapshot_abstract: The demand for powerful instruction following and reasoning capability of large language models (LLMs) has promoted rapid development of retrieval-augmented generation (RAG). The RAG system assists LLM generation by retrieving chunks of query-fit supplementary knowledge from an external database. Conventional RAG systems, however, suffer from information insufficiency due to two factors, which are intent-agnostic retrieval and information fragmentation. Our work proposes a RAG framework, termed InSemRAG, that addresses these challenges via an iterative retrieve-and-check mechanism with two supporting modules, an intention-aware retriever (IAR) and semantics-preserving chunking (SPC). IAR implements a dynamic hybrid retrieval method that adaptively weights the retrieval channels based on the query intent, while SPC performs detection and reparation to the damaged evidence chunks to preserve the semantic integrity. To alleviate the computational latency brought by our iterative mechanism, we leverage small language models (SLMs). Extensive experiments across several benchmark datasets consistently demonstrate the competitiveness of our method against recent state-of-the-art RAG mechanisms. Particularly, our method achieves significant gains on multi-hop and evidence-sensitive tasks, with a 2.65-point improvement in F1 on HotPotQA and a 1.5-point increase in accuracy on FEVER. Our method also achieves competitive performance to Multi-Hop RAG with 4.32$\times$ lower latency with the utilization of SLM.

actions.title: 在论文中讨论Power of Noise实验敏感性
actions.rationale: 新论文发现RAG中的“噪声之力”效应高度依赖实验设置，该效应可在小幅改动下出现、减弱或消失，表明实验设计对RAG稳健性结论影响巨大。这与我们“RAG系统对扰动异常敏感”的发现形成潜在呼应，但因任务、数据集和度量标准完全不兼容，无法直接支持或质疑我们的结论。因此，我们应在相关工作中加入讨论，点明这种实验敏感性的普遍性，并谨慎说明两者间的背景差异。
checklist: 加入精确引用
说明条件差异
避免把跨条件结果写成直接支持或反驳
更新 Discussion/Limitations
精读新论文，梳理其关于实验设置（提示格式、解码限制、模型版本）如何影响“噪声之力”效应的具体发现。
在手稿“相关工作”或讨论节中撰写一段，说明该研究揭示了RAG稳健性评估对实验设计的高度敏感性，与我们的扰动敏感性发现可能存在共同根源。
明确声明因任务（QA vs. 稳健性评估）、数据集（NQ vs. RARE-Set）和度量（准确率 vs. RARE-Met）差异，该证据不可直接比较，仅作为边界条件参考。
将新论文引用加入文献列表，并在正文中准确插入引用标记。
通读修改后的段落，确保未夸大可比性或误解该论文的核心结论。
actions.source_title: The Powerless Noise: How Experimental Settings Shape the Reported Power of Noise

actions.title: 在相关工作部分引用InSemRAG并说明差异
actions.rationale: InSemRAG通过在HotpotQA上提升F1分数2.65点、FEVER准确率1.5点，展示了意图感知检索和语义保持分块对RAG鲁棒性的改进。尽管其任务、数据集、指标与本研究的鲁棒性评估设定不直接可比，但该工作表明不同RAG配置下敏感性问题的普遍性，值得作为前置研究加以引用，以支撑本论文关于RAG脆弱性的论述。
checklist: 精读MIRAGE论文，理解其防御机制与实验条件。
在文稿“相关工作”部分添加MIRAGE引用，说明其针对长文本检索污染提出防御方案。
简要讨论MIRAGE与本文在任务、数据上的差异，突出互补性。
更新参考文献列表，确保格式一致。
通读全文，检查术语如“鲁棒性”使用的一致性。
在论文的“相关工作”或“讨论”部分定位合适段落，用于介绍RAG鲁棒性相关的前置工作。
撰写1–2句文字，客观描述InSemRAG的核心方法（意图感知检索与语义保持分块）及其在鲁棒性任务上的主要结果。
明确指出两者在任务设定、数据集、评价指标等方面的差异，避免读者误解为直接可比。
将引用格式化为目标期刊或会议的文献条目，并插入正文中。
actions.source_title: Efficient RAG with Intent-Aware Retrieval and Semantics-Preserving Chunking

output.title: RARE: Retrieval-Aware Robustness Evaluation for Retrieval-Augmented Generation Systems
output.research_problem: How robust are RAG systems under query, document, and retrieval perturbations?
output.central_thesis: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems are unexpectedly sensitive to perturbations, and their robustness does not always scale with model size or query complexity.
contributions: Proposes RARE, a unified Retrieval-Aware Robustness Evaluation framework that jointly stress-tests queries, documents, and retrieval perturbations over dynamic, time-sensitive corpora.
Introduces RARE-Get, an automated knowledge-graph-driven synthesis pipeline that constructs single-hop and multi-hop QA pairs from domain-specific documents without manual curation.
Constructs RARE-Set, a large-scale benchmark spanning 527 expert-level documents and 48,295 questions across finance, economics, and policy domains with evolving distributions.
Formalizes RARE-Met, retrieval-conditioned robustness metrics that capture a model's ability to remain correct or recover under systematic query, document, and real-world retrieval perturbations.
methods: Knowledge graph extraction and normalization using LLMs (GPT-4.1) and embedding models (E5-Mistral-7B-Instruct).
Pattern-based knowledge graph traversal to generate single-hop and multi-hop (chained, star, inverted-star) query-answer pairs.
LLM-based quality assurance filtering on readability, clarity, and correctness using Claude 3.5 Haiku.
Systematic query perturbations: character-level noise, word-level substitutions, grammar rewrites, and irrelevant information insertion via TextAttack and LLMs.
Document perturbations: regex-based answer removal, back-translation, and real-world retrieval simulation via LangChain with re-ranking.
Open-ended answer evaluation: two-stage pipeline (string matching and Claude-3-Haiku semantic judgment) to compare predictions against ground truth.
Robustness metrics defined conditionally on parametric knowledge probe g(q,∅).
datasets: 527 expert-level time-sensitive finance (199 S&P 500 10-K filings), economics (114 OECD surveys), and policy (214 CAPER reports) documents
48,295 QA pairs spanning single-hop and multi-hop (chained, star, inverted-star) questions
Experiments use a 6,000 QA subset (1,000 single-hop + 1,000 multi-hop per domain)
evaluation_protocol: Measure overall robustness, query robustness, document robustness, and retrieval robustness as defined in Table 4.
Retrieval uses top embedding models (E5-Large-Instruct, Jina-Embedding-v3, Stella-En-1.5B-v5).
Generators include open-source (Llama 3.2 family, Qwen 3 family) and proprietary models (GPT-4.1 series) with deterministic decoding (temperature=0).
Correctness judged via string matching and LLM (Claude-3-Haiku) semantic comparison.
Statistical significance tested with pairwise two-proportion z-tests.
key_findings: RAG systems show unexpected sensitivity to query, document, and retrieval perturbations (core).
Robustness scores do not always scale strictly with model size: some mid-sized generators like Qwen3-8B outperform larger counterparts like Qwen3-32B.
Document robustness shows regression or stagnation with increasing model size in some families; smaller models may safely refuse while larger models hallucinate.
Single-hop queries consistently yield higher robustness scores than multi-hop queries across all domains.
Robustness varies significantly across domains (finance highest, economics lowest).
Current models are less sensitive to query perturbations than to document perturbations.
limitations: Experiments limited to a subset of 6,000 QA pairs (1k per domain × type) from the full 48,295 set.
Evaluation relies on LLM judges which may introduce bias; string matching and Claude-3-Haiku used to mitigate.
Robustness metrics depend on the parametric knowledge probe g(q,∅) being accurate.
Perturbations, while systematic, may not cover all real-world noise types.
Retriever and generator combinations limited to selected models; performance may vary with other choices.
Focus only on English-language, domain-specific (finance/economics/policy) documents; generalizability to other languages/domains untested.
terminology: Single-hop and multi-hop (chained, star, inverted-star) query patterns
Lexical relevance vs. answer relevance in document perturbations

claim_profiles.claim_summary: Using the RARE-Get pipeline, we construct RARE-Set with 527 expert-level time-sensitive documents and 48,295 questions whose distribution evolves as sources change.

contract.scope: 527 documents, 48,295 questions across finance, economics, policy; time-sensitive with dynamic updates

boundary_conditions: Pipeline relies on LLM extraction quality (GPT-4.1) and embedding-based relation normalization.
Quality filtering removes pairs with scores < 3 on readability/clarity/correctness.
Multi-hop patterns constrained to chained, star, and inverted-star shapes.
Documents must be segmented into ~600-token chunks with domain-specific rules.
falsification_tests: If a similar pipeline applied to the same corpus fails to reproduce the number and diversity of QA pairs, the claim is challenged.
If human evaluation of a random sample shows high error rates in correctness or multi-hop necessity, the claim's validity is weakened.

claim_profiles.claim_summary: Document robustness score does not significantly improve with model size; some models even show regression, likely because larger models hallucinate rather than refuse.

contract.comparator: across model sizes (Llama 3.2 1B/3B/11B/90B, Qwen3 4B/8B/32B, GPT-4.1 nano/mini/full)
contract.scope: Finance, economics, policy domains; document perturbations: lexical-sim without answer, lexical-diff with answer, ground truth, real-world retrieval

boundary_conditions: Robustness defined conditionally on g(q,∅) probe.
Document perturbation types limited to back-translation and answer removal.
Evaluated on 6,000 QA sample; not the full 48k.
Models run with temperature=0.
Findings based on specific embedding model choices (not varied).
falsification_tests: A replication with different document perturbation methods that shows strong positive correlation between size and document robustness would challenge this.
If a new larger model (e.g., 200B+) achieves significantly higher document robustness with similar refusal patterns, the trend may not hold.

claim_profiles.claim_summary: Single-hop queries consistently yield higher robustness scores than multi-hop queries across all domains and perturbation types.

contract.scope: All three domains (finance, economics, policy); across all query, document, and retrieval perturbations

boundary_conditions: Multi-hop patterns restricted to three specific graph traversal patterns.
Evaluation uses the subset of 1,000 single-hop and 1,000 multi-hop per domain (6,000 total).
Perturbation types as defined in RARE-Met.
Generators evaluated include both open and closed models.
falsification_tests: A study using a different multi-hop QA generation method that produces questions of comparable difficulty but where multi-hop robustness equals or exceeds single-hop would challenge this.
Testing with models specifically optimized for multi-hop reasoning that achieve parity would weaken the claim.

claim_profiles.claim_summary: RAG systems are unexpectedly sensitive to perturbations (queries, documents, real-world retrieval).

contract.scope: Finance, economics, policy; all perturbation types

boundary_conditions: Findings averaged across multiple model families (Llama, Qwen, GPT) and sizes.
Perturbation types defined by RARE (surface and semantic query changes, document relevance variants, real retrieval noise).
Temperature=0, max output 1024 tokens.
Evaluation via string match + Claude-3-Haiku.
falsification_tests: If a systematically constructed RAG system (e.g., with robust components) achieves >95% robustness under the same perturbations, the 'unexpectedly sensitive' conclusion is challenged.
If similar experiments on other dynamic benchmarks show high robustness, the claim's domain/benchmark specificity may be exposed.

claim_profiles.claim_summary: Robustness scores do not always scale strictly with model size; some mid-sized generators outperform several larger counterparts.

contract.scope: Qwen3 family (4B, 8B, 32B), Llama 3.2 family (1B, 3B, 11B, 90B), GPT-4.1 series

boundary_conditions: Models from different families trained on different data/methodologies.
Parameter count is used as a proxy for size; architectural differences are not normalized.
The 6,000 QA subset focuses on three complex, domain-specific corpora.
Refusal behavior is part of robustness definition (g(q,∅) probe).
falsification_tests: Testing future models in the same family where monotonic scaling holds across all robustness dimensions would challenge the non-monotonic observation.
A controlled experiment where only model size varies (same architecture, training data) that shows strict scaling would question the finding's generality.

cases.title: RadarNet: Robust Retrieval Under Domain Shift
cases.research_question: How can retrieval-augmented systems remain accurate, calibrated, and efficient under domain shift?

revisions.statement: RadarNet improves exact match over BM25 under unseen-domain evaluation.
revisions.falsifiable_condition: A matched evaluation fails to show an exact-match improvement over BM25.
revisions.source_quote: On DomainQA, RadarNet improves exact match from 61.2% to 68.7% over BM25 under unseen-domain evaluation.

contract.scope: RadarNet

revisions.statement: RadarNet is robust to 20% retrieval noise.
revisions.falsifiable_condition: Exact match degrades materially under 20% evidence noise.
revisions.source_quote: RadarNet maintains 66.4% exact match when 20% of evidence passages contain retrieval noise.

contract.scope: 20% evidence noise

revisions.statement: Hierarchical routing reduces retrieval latency versus a flat dense index.
revisions.falsifiable_condition: The hierarchical router does not reduce matched median latency.
revisions.source_quote: The hierarchical evidence router reduces median retrieval latency by 31% compared with a flat dense index.

contract.scope: hierarchical router

revisions.statement: Calibrated abstention lowers unsupported answers on ShiftBench.
revisions.falsifiable_condition: Matched abstention evaluation does not lower unsupported-answer rate.
revisions.source_quote: Our calibrated abstention rule lowers unsupported-answer rate from 14.8% to 6.1% on ShiftBench.

contract.scope: RadarNet calibrated abstention

revisions.statement: Source-diverse batches improve low-resource domain performance.
revisions.falsifiable_condition: Macro-F1 does not improve across matched low-resource domains.
revisions.source_quote: Training with source-diverse batches improves Macro-F1 by 4.2 points across three low-resource domains.

contract.scope: source-diverse training

revisions.statement: Claim-aware reranking contributes substantially to exact match.
revisions.falsifiable_condition: Removing reranking does not reduce exact match.
revisions.source_quote: Ablation results show that removing claim-aware reranking decreases exact match by 5.6 points.

contract.scope: RadarNet

revisions.statement: RadarNet outperforms ColBERT-v2 on the DomainQA temporal split.
revisions.falsifiable_condition: A matched evaluation does not outperform ColBERT-v2.
revisions.source_quote: RadarNet outperforms ColBERT-v2 by 3.9 exact-match points on the temporal split of DomainQA.

contract.scope: RadarNet

revisions.statement: The uncertainty head is well calibrated on the institutional split.
revisions.falsifiable_condition: Expected calibration error is not low on the held-out institutional split.
revisions.source_quote: The uncertainty head reaches an expected calibration error of 0.041 on the held-out institutional split.

contract.scope: uncertainty head

revisions.statement: The evaluation relies on CleanEval relevance judgments.
revisions.falsifiable_condition: The released relevance judgments are invalid or compromised.
revisions.source_quote: Our evaluation protocol follows the CleanEval benchmark and uses its released relevance judgments.

contract.scope: evaluation protocol

revisions.statement: The full system reaches 120 queries per second on one A100.
revisions.falsifiable_condition: Matched hardware and batching do not reproduce the reported throughput.
revisions.source_quote: The full system processes 120 queries per second on a single A100 GPU with batch size 32.

contract.scope: single A100, batch size 32

evidence_own.quote: On DomainQA, RadarNet improves exact match from 61.2% to 68.7% over BM25 under unseen-domain evaluation.

evidence_new.quote: On the DomainQA unseen-domain split, EchoRAG reaches 70.1 exact match while our RadarNet reproduction reaches 64.0 using the same BM25 baseline.

impacts.claim_statement: RadarNet improves exact match over BM25 under unseen-domain evaluation.
impacts.source_snapshot_abstract: EchoRAG evaluates retrieval-augmented question answering under domain shift. On the DomainQA unseen-domain split, EchoRAG reaches 70.1 exact match while our RadarNet reproduction reaches 64.0 using the same BM25 baseline. We release evaluation logs for comparison.

evidence_own.quote: RadarNet maintains 66.4% exact match when 20% of evidence passages contain retrieval noise.

evidence_new.quote: With 20% retrieval noise on DomainQA, RadarNet retains 65.9% exact match across five seeds.

impacts.claim_statement: RadarNet is robust to 20% retrieval noise.

source.title: Noise stress tests for retrieval-augmented QA

impacts.source_snapshot_abstract: With 20% retrieval noise on DomainQA, RadarNet retains 65.9% exact match across five seeds. This independent stress test supports robustness to corrupted evidence passages.

evidence_own.quote: The hierarchical evidence router reduces median retrieval latency by 31% compared with a flat dense index.

evidence_new.quote: LayerRoute reduces median retrieval latency by 34% relative to a flat dense index on DomainQA.

uncertainty: Independent implementation; scientific stance is not inferred from competitor status.
impacts.claim_statement: Hierarchical routing reduces retrieval latency versus a flat dense index.
impacts.source_snapshot_abstract: LayerRoute reduces median retrieval latency by 34% relative to a flat dense index on DomainQA. Nova Retrieval Lab introduces a hierarchical router designed for low-latency evidence retrieval.

evidence_own.quote: Our calibrated abstention rule lowers unsupported-answer rate from 14.8% to 6.1% on ShiftBench.

evidence_new.quote: On the ShiftBench test split, RadarNet's calibrated abstention produces a 12.9% unsupported-answer rate, while the matched no-abstention system produces 14.7%.

impacts.claim_statement: Calibrated abstention lowers unsupported answers on ShiftBench.
impacts.source_snapshot_abstract: We reassess calibrated abstention using the published ShiftBench protocol. On the ShiftBench test split, RadarNet's calibrated abstention produces a 12.9% unsupported-answer rate, while the matched no-abstention system produces 14.7%. Calibration choices explain much of the variance.

condition_differences.explanation: At least one source does not report this condition.

evidence_own.quote: Training with source-diverse batches improves Macro-F1 by 4.2 points across three low-resource domains.

evidence_new.quote: Source-diverse batches improve Macro-F1 for news but show no improvement for the biomedical domain; the evaluation split is not reported.

uncertainty: Incoming split is not reported.
impacts.claim_statement: Source-diverse batches improve low-resource domain performance.

source.title: Where source diversity helps domain adaptation

impacts.source_snapshot_abstract: Source-diverse batches improve Macro-F1 for news but show no improvement for the biomedical domain; the evaluation split is not reported. These results suggest a domain-specific boundary rather than a universal benefit.

evidence_own.quote: Ablation results show that removing claim-aware reranking decreases exact match by 5.6 points.

evidence_new.quote: Removing claim-aware reranking from RadarNet reduces DomainQA exact match by 5.2 points in our independent implementation.

impacts.claim_statement: Claim-aware reranking contributes substantially to exact match.
impacts.source_snapshot_abstract: Removing claim-aware reranking from RadarNet reduces DomainQA exact match by 5.2 points in our independent implementation. The effect is stable across seeds.

condition_differences.explanation: At least one source does not report this condition.

evidence_own.quote: Our evaluation protocol follows the CleanEval benchmark and uses its released relevance judgments.

evidence_new.quote: The CleanEval relevance judgments are retracted after leakage between annotation and evaluation sets was confirmed.

uncertainty: The downstream magnitude of the integrity issue has not yet been measured.
impacts.claim_statement: The evaluation relies on CleanEval relevance judgments.

source.title: Retraction notice for CleanEval relevance judgments

impacts.source_snapshot_abstract: The CleanEval relevance judgments are retracted after leakage between annotation and evaluation sets was confirmed. Results depending on the released judgments should be revalidated.

contract.scope: Reported on entire test sets; 13% accuracy gain and 37% search reduction computed over combined results.

revisions.statement: Early practices typically employ a twostage process: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) first generate visual captions, followed by text-based retrieval through LLMs [14].

Notably, the architec-
ture operates effectively with an 8B parameter model, substantially

ning approaches.

contract.comparator: Existing planning approaches (implicitly larger models like OmniSearch’s iterative planner or other agent architectures)
contract.scope: Planner model size: InternVL2-8B (8 billion parameters) vs. other planners that may require larger models.

revisions.statement: In summary, our contributions are threefold: • E-Agent Framework: A novel plan-then-execute architecture combining a dynamic mRAG planner with a tool-aware executor, achieving state-of-the-art performance in VQA tasks through optimized multimodal retrieval workflows. • RemPlan Benchmark : The first comprehensive evaluation framework for mRAG systems, featuring retrievaldependent/-independent questions with expert-validated plans and disentangled evaluation protocols. • Empirical Validation: Extensive experiments demonstrating improvements of VQA ability improvements alongside considerable reduction in redundant searches, supported by systematic analysis studies.
revisions.falsifiable_condition: A matched evaluation does not reproduce the reported result.
revisions.source_quote:
In summary, our contributions are threefold:
• E-Agent Framework: A novel plan-then-execute architec-
ture combining a dynamic mRAG planner with a tool-aware
executor, achieving state-of-the-art performance in VQA
tasks through optimized multimodal retrieval workflows.
• RemPlan Benchmark : The first comprehensive evalu-
ation framework for mRAG systems, featuring retrieval-
dependent/-independent questions with expert-validated
plans and disentangled evaluation protocols.
• Empirical Validation: Extensive experiments demonstrat-
ing improvements of VQA ability improvements alongside
considerable reduction in redundant searches, supported
by systematic analysis studies.

revisions.statement: All tool configurations receive explicit representation during the training process of mRAG planner. 5 Experiments 5.1 Datasets and Metrics To comprehensively evaluate the performance of the proposed method, we conducted experiments on the RemPlan benchmark as well as three related datasets: the MMSearch benchmark [9], Dyn-VQA [13], and A-OKVQA [20].

training process of mRAG planner.

method, we conducted experiments on the RemPlan benchmark as
well as three related datasets: the MMSearch benchmark [9], Dyn-

source.title: When Do Multimodal and Graph-Augmented RAG Help? A Controlled Evaluation for Document Question Answering

impacts.source_snapshot_abstract: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems commonly operate on text extracted from documents, potentially losing information contained in figures, tables, layout, and relationships distributed across passages. We present an explainable multimodal graph-RAG architecture that augments a text-only baseline with LLM-extracted subject--relation--object triples and CLIP-based retrieval of figures and tables. The three evidence sources are retrieved independently and fused only at generation time, allowing the effects of graph evidence, visual evidence, and generator choice to be evaluated separately. We conduct a controlled four-way ablation on 1,000 PubLayNet pages across single-passage, multi-hop, and figure questions using two closed-weight and two open-weight multimodal generators. We also compare matched caption-answerable and pixel-only figure question sets to distinguish caption recovery from genuine visual question answering. Knowledge-graph augmentation provides no reliable accuracy improvement across generators or question types in this corpus. On pixel-only questions, text-only systems achieve zero accuracy, while multimodal systems reach 0.057--0.114 and are constrained by both image retrieval (Recall@3 = 0.371) and the generators' ability to interpret dense scientific figures and tables. Caption-derived questions substantially overestimate text-only visual question-answering capability. Processing the same image also produces an elevenfold variation in input tokens across generators, showing that image tokenisation can dominate deployment cost. Source code, experiment configurations, and evaluation resources are publicly available at https://github.com/sokistar24/multimodal-graph-rag. These findings show that the value of multimodal and graph augmentation depends on retrieval design, corpus structure, benchmark construction, and generator efficiency.

source.title: PIXELRAG: Web Screenshots Beat Text for Retrieval-Augmented Generation

impacts.source_snapshot_abstract: Augmenting large language models (LLMs) with retrieved web text has become a dominant paradigm, yet the web is not natively textual: existing systems depend on complex parsing pipelines that linearize HTML and discard layout, visual structure, and formatting. We introduce PixelRAG, a new retrieval-augmented method that represents websites in their native visual form and performs retrieval and reading entirely in pixel space, enabling an end-to-end architecture that eliminates text abstraction. PixelRAG is, to our knowledge, the first pipeline to operate over a full Wikipedia corpus in this form, scaling to a datastore of 30 million screenshot images with an efficient visual retrieval index. Built on an existing visual embedding model (i.e., Qwen3-VL-Embedding), PixelRAG further fine-tunes this model on screenshot data with carefully curated contrastive training data. Retrieved screenshots are then fed directly as pixel inputs to a VLM, without intermediate text conversion. PixelRAG consistently outperforms both no-retrieval and text-based RAG baselines, most surprisingly on widely studied text-centric tasks such as NQ and SimpleQA. It also achieves strong gains on multimodal open-domain QA (e.g., MMSearch), benchmarks over noisy news corpora (e.g., LiveVQA), and agentic benchmarks (e.g., MoNaCo), improving accuracy by up to 18.1% over text-based baselines. Finally, pixel representations enable a new efficiency lever for RAG through image compression, achieving up to 3x token cost reduction at lower resolutions while maintaining accuracy. Our results challenge the necessity of text representations in web retrieval, suggesting that web RAG can operate directly in the web's native visual form while improving both performance and efficiency.

impacts.claim_statement: Experiments across RemPlan and three established benchmarks show that E-Agent improves accuracy by 13% over state-of-the-art mRAG methods while reducing redundant searches by 37%.

source.title: Very Efficient Listwise Multimodal Reranking for Long Documents

impacts.source_snapshot_abstract: Listwise reranking is a key yet computationally expensive component in vision-centric retrieval and multimodal retrieval-augmented generation (M-RAG) over long documents. While recent VLM-based rerankers achieve strong accuracy, their practicality is often limited by long visual-token sequences and multi-step autoregressive decoding. We propose ZipRerank, a highly efficient listwise multimodal reranker that directly addresses both bottlenecks. It reduces input length via a lightweight query-image early interaction mechanism and eliminates autoregressive decoding by scoring all candidates in a single forward pass. To enable effective learning, ZipRerank adopts a two-stage training strategy: (i) listwise pretraining on large-scale text data rendered as images, and (ii) multimodal finetuning with VLM-teacher-distilled soft-ranking supervision. Extensive experiments on the MMDocIR benchmark show that ZipRerank matches or surpasses state-of-the-art multimodal rerankers while reducing LLM inference latency by up to an order of magnitude, making it well-suited for latency-sensitive real-world systems. The code is available at https://github.com/dukesun99/ZipRerank.

actions.title: 区分定位并关注：Very Efficient Listwise Multimodal Reranking for Long Documents 与 C1 条件不可直接比较
actions.rationale: Very Efficient Listwise Multimodal Reranking for Long Documents 在不同任务、数据或指标设置下考察了与 C1 相邻的假设；当前证据不能直接支持或反驳你的主张，建议在 related work 中明确区分定位，并评估是否值得增补一个条件匹配的对照实验。
checklist: 核对双方任务、数据集、指标与 split 的差异
在 related work 中说明设置差异与定位区别
评估是否增补一个条件匹配的对照实验
持续关注该工作的后续可比结果

output.title: E-Agent 多模态检索规划
output.research_problem: Single-pass planning and dynamic tool orchestration for improving multimodal RAG accuracy while reducing redundant search.
output.central_thesis: A one-time dynamic mRAG planning strategy, decoupling planning from execution, improves VQA accuracy by 13% over SOTA while reducing redundant searches by 37%, using an 8B parameter model.
contributions: E-Agent Framework: A plan-then-execute architecture with a dynamic mRAG planner and tool-aware executor.
RemPlan Benchmark: First comprehensive benchmark for mRAG planning with retrieval-dependent/-independent questions and plan annotations.
Empirical Validation: Extensive experiments showing VQA accuracy improvements and 37% reduction in redundant searches.
methods: One-time mRAG planning via a fine-tuned InternVL2-8B planner.
Dynamic tool selection orchestrated by the planner.
Task executor that invokes search tools (image search, text search) and MLLM tools (requery, response).
evaluation_protocol: Answer quality (Ans.) scored by GPT-4o (0-2 range).
Plan evaluation metrics: Tool-Specific Precision and Recall (IS-P, IS-R, TS-P, TS-R), Plan Accuracy (Plan-acc), Parameter Correctness (Param-acc), Semantic Similarity (Param-sim).
Average number of tool calls (search tools, MLLM, mRAG planner).
Pearson correlation between GPT-4o Ans. and human evaluation.
key_findings: E-Agent outperforms SOTA mRAG methods on all question types in RemPlan.
On Type 1 questions (no search needed), E-Agent avoids noise from unnecessary searches.
E-Agent reduces tool invocations significantly compared to MMSearch and OmniSearch.
Fine-tuned E-Agent generalizes to out-of-distribution benchmarks (Dyn-VQA, MMSearch).
On A-OKVQA, E-Agent matches baseline MLLM while other mRAG methods degrade.
GPT-4o answer scoring correlates well with human evaluation (Pearson >0.69).
limitations: Challenges with complex multi-hop reasoning requiring iterative plan refinement.
Dependence on predefined toolkits, needing updates for evolving data sources.
Search result quality directly impacts VQA performance (e.g., case where image search failed).
terminology: One-time planning: Single-pass plan generation without iterative refinement
Plan-then-execute: Decoupled planning and execution architecture

claim_profiles.claim_summary: E-Agent improves accuracy by 13% over SOTA mRAG methods and reduces redundant searches by 37% on RemPlan and three benchmarks.

contract.task: Visual Question Answering (VQA) in multimodal RAG settings
contract.metric: Ans. score (GPT-4o evaluated, 0-2 range) and search tool call counts
contract.comparator: State-of-the-art mRAG methods: Qwen2-VL-72B, MMSearch, OmniSearch
contract.scope: Reported on entire test sets; 13% accuracy gain and 37% search reduction computed over combined results.

boundary_conditions: MLLM backbone: Qwen2-VL-72B
Planner model: InternVL2-8B fine-tuned on 10k plan-annotated samples
Search tools: Baidu Image Search, Tavily text search
Evaluation metrics include plan accuracy and tool precision/recall
Redundant search reduction measured as decrease in average number of search tool calls vs. comparators
Ans. metric validated by human correlation
falsification_tests: Reproduce E-Agent with same planner, MLLM, and tools; measure Ans. score on RemPlan and tool calls; compare to reported values (E-Agent-sft Ans.=1.25, search calls=1.05).
Replace planner with a random or static tool selector; check if accuracy drops and search calls increase.
Evaluate on a new mRAG benchmark with similar question types; verify if 13% gain and 37% reduction hold.
Use a different MLLM backbone; test if gains persist (boundary condition of using Qwen2-VL-72B).

claim_profiles.claim_summary: E-Agent operates effectively with an 8B parameter model, substantially lowering computational requirements compared to existing planning approaches.

contract.comparator: Existing planning approaches (implicitly larger models like OmniSearch’s iterative planner or other agent architectures)
contract.scope: Planner model size: InternVL2-8B (8 billion parameters) vs. other planners that may require larger models.

boundary_conditions: Planner is InternVL2-8B, fine-tuned with 10k samples
MLLM for answer generation is Qwen2-VL-72B (separate from planner)
Effectiveness demonstrated by achieving SOTA performance with only one-shot planning call
Lower computational requirements refer to reduced inference overhead from single-pass planning vs. iterative planning
falsification_tests: Compare E-Agent's planner inference cost (FLOPs, latency) against OmniSearch’s iterative planner on the same hardware when answering RemPlan questions.
Train an E-Agent planner with even smaller model (e.g., 2B) and see if performance holds; if accuracy drops significantly, claim may be sensitive to model size.
Measure total mRAG planner calls (Table 3: E-Agent-sft requires 1.00 call vs. OmniSearch 2.96); verify that the 8B model indeed achieves this efficiency.

claim_profiles.claim_summary: E-Agent achieves state-of-the-art VQA performance on all four types of questions in the RemPlan benchmark.

contract.metric: Ans. score (GPT-4o, 0-2) per question type (Type 1-4) and overall
contract.scope: All four question types in RemPlan test set

boundary_conditions: Evaluation uses GPT-4o scoring validated against human judgment
Comparators are reproduced with identical MLLM backbone and search tools
E-Agent-sft variant used for claim
Ans. scores: Type1=1.65, Type2=1.17, Type3=1.00, Type4=0.89, Overall=1.25
falsification_tests: Reproduce E-Agent-sft and comparators on RemPlan; verify that E-Agent-sft's Ans. scores are strictly higher than all comparators for each question type.
Introduce a new strong baseline (e.g., a variant of OmniSearch with better search strategies); check if E-Agent still leads on all four types.
Evaluate on a different set of questions sampled from RemPlan distribution; confirm performance dominance across types.
